title: The Macro Minute: Should investors chase the bearish trend in the US dollar?

Happy Tuesday out there team42. It's
your skipper here Darius Dell to present
our macro minute for Tuesday January
27th 2026. As always we'll start with
the executive summary from today's
leadoff warning note. So let's dive
right in. Today's key macro question is
should investors chase the bearish trend
in the US dollar? The short answer is
yes. The US dollar, which recently broke
down to bearish from the perspective of
our volatility just a momentum signal,
is overvalued and features a myriad of
cyclical and structural headwinds that
should durably weigh on its price versus
peer fiat currencies in gold. Terms of
analytical nuance, the US dollar
following its steep steepest weekly loss
since May slid to its weakest level
since 2022 as a forest downturn
accelerated amid growing evidence of US
efforts to backs stop the Japanese yen.
There are those in the market that see
additional pressure on the US dollar
coming from uncertainty surrounding
President Trump's economic and
geopolitical policy agenda which
includes a forthcoming nomination for
Fed chair. We on record many times
confirming our support for our friend
Rick Reer of Black Rockck, who in our
view represents far and away the best
choice to guide the institution and
global financial markets through the
forth turning side structural reforms
the institution must make to counter the
growing geopolitically driven supply
demand imbalance in the Treasury bond
market. In case you missed it, I was
featured in Barons today uh on on Rick
Reer's candidacy. So, uh, we have to
link the note, uh, here and we tweeted
the note out, uh, on Twitter, uh, and
and posted it on LinkedIn. So,
definitely take make time to review that
if you haven't had a chance. Uh,
reminder, the US dollar is historically
strong on a nominal effective exchange
rate basis. The US dollar is also
historically strong on a real effective
exchange rate basis. A geopolitical
policy discount has emerged in the US
dollar, which is no longer tightly
tracking US interest rates since
liberation day. The dollar is
appropriately priced from a real
interest rate differential perspective.
However, which suggests our structural
bears bias will prove precient if we're
right on structural reforms at the Fed.
FYI, our structural reforms at the Fed
thesis is far from far from being
adequately priced, which implies much
more downside in the US dollar over the
long term. European and Japanese
investors are already losing money by
investing in treasuries and hedging out
the risk of US dollar depreciation. A
sustained decline in the greenback will
only exacerbate the growing
geopolitically driven supply demand
imbalance in the Treasury bond market,
which will reflexively force the Fed to
supply greater and greater quantities of
financial repression and monetary
debasement to offset it. The Fed is
currently priced to be the most ma uh
doubbish major central bank over the
next year. The relatively doubbish Fed
reaction function should continue to
weigh on the dollar over the medium
term. Reserve management purchases are
likely to weigh on the dollar as well.
The Fed's balance sheet is now expanding
5% on a month- over-month annualized
basis, which is the second fastest
expansion among major central banks
behind the PBOC. As always, members of
our global investor community can trust
that our institutional-grade risk
management overlays, KISS and Dr. Mo,
will help our portfolios navigate these
emergent risks better than investors
attempting to manage risk without
systematic rules or relying on
fundamental research alone. Transition
to our 42 micro dashboard. As always, I
wrap up with a question from our
community. This one's titled uh stocks
and gold or spxg says, "Hi DD 42 micro
community. Thank you for your research
and helping me navigate this market
regime. I'm very grateful for the
confidence KISS provided when I first uh
joined and allocating my entire
portfolio. It has been a strong uh
second half of 2025 from when I joined.
My question is regarding the uh S&P GLD
ratio. We're at a point similar to 1973
and 2008 where SBXGLD had weekly closes
below the 1617 range and later led to a
strong decline in stocks. Although we're
it green, it seems GLD growth continues
to outpace XPX. Would it would would
this be something you're concerned
about? Since yesterday uh since
yesterday's uh little morning note
indicated reflation, would you expect
the S&P SPX to outperform gold in the
short to medium term since it seems to
favor risk assets or is this just noise
or is this anybody's guess and a dumb
question? So, uh excellent question.
Thank you for the very kind feedback
regarding KISS. That's is what exactly
what we built it for. So, the first
thing I'll say is the speed with which
gold is appreciating in price, or rather
the speed with which the dollar is being
devalued versus the thing that's been
money for 6,000 years as opposed to the
greenback which has only been around for
a couple hundred years. The speed with
which the dollar is being devalued
versus gold should freak you out. And
I'm not trying to freak you out, but you
should be concerned. Let's be totally
honest. The dollar is down [laughter]
70 80% year-over-year versus the thing
that has been money for 6,000 years now.
Historically speaking, we've had these
significant currency devaluations versus
gold, it tends to not lead to great
outcomes. Total war tends to be a very
high probability outcome whenever you
see u changes uh currencies get devalued
versus gold to this degree. Uh and and
that's partially what central banks are
probably preparing for is that you know
we're moving into a multipolar world and
a multipolar world is a world with
increased friction from a trade and
economic standpoint and that increased
friction from a trade and economic
standpoint should increase friction from
a geopolitical standpoint in terms of um
in terms of you know the risk increased
risk of violent conflict. Now again not
making these predictions we're just
telling you that this is the kind of
stuff that historically has occurred in
four turnings according to our
statistical research and and and the
research of others like Peter Turin
readalio etc. Uh and so ultimately you
must be aware that hey we're moving into
a different regime, different monetary
regime perhaps, different geopolitical
regime perhaps and ultimately this is
the movement from regime A to regime B
has historically not necessarily been
great you know for stocks. Whenever you
have structural regime change it tends
not to be great for the legacy positions
in financial markets whether it be in
the stock market or the bond market etc.
Uh I'm less concerned about a secular
bare market in stocks from here because
I think we're from a sequence
perspective in terms of respecting the
x-axxis. From a sequence perspective, I
think we're still in the early innings
of investor consensus agreeing to you
know realizing what we've already
realized which is in the US is currently
experiencing a structural upturn uptrend
rather in productivity growth. And if
we're right on that uh the durability of
that structural uptrend and productivity
growth, that means you probably have
more uh right tail risk to price in from
a equity market standpoint. However,
we've also been very uh consistent uh in
this program and in our research that
that we're highly likely to see a
secular bare market in stocks once this
market cycle peaks. One, we have a
historic degree of crowd bullish
positioning. So that amplifies that risk
to begin with. But secondarily,
historically, what we've seen in these
major capex bubbles going back to the
1800s, you know, the railroad capex
bubble of the 1860, 1850s, 1870 uh uh
1870s, we saw the consumer durable goods
capex bubble in the 1920s, and then
obviously the internet capex bubble in
the 1990s, and now we're here with the
AI capex bubble here in the 2020s.
The previous three capex bubbles all led
to uh structural bare markets uh in
stocks or secular bare markets in
stocks. And so, uh, I do think that is a
reasonably high probability risk for
whenever this market cycle peaks, the
stock market cycle peaks, uh, which is,
you know, essentially what the, um, the
stock, the XPX GLD ratio may be
signaling from a medium to longer term
perspective. But ultimately, I still
think we're too, it's too, we're too
early innings in consensus figuring out
what we've already figured out with
regarding productivity growth. Once
consensus figures that out and, you
know, we're at a different, you know,
plane in terms of, uh, earnings
expectations and valuations and credit
spreads, etc., then that's when you
could really start to have some u some
issues associated with the investors
that are supplying all that capital to
the AI capex bubble. They may they're
going [clears throat] to start to want
their money back and that's usually what
um that's usually what triggers the uh
the unwind of the of the bubble. So uh
we'll wrap it up there. Jerry still here
presenting our macro minute for Tuesday,
January 27th, 2026. Best of luck out
there today. We'll catch you back here
tomorrow. Cheers.
